transcript: Welcome to Lesson 6.9, Email and SMS Analytics, What to Track and Why. Over the past 8 lessons, you have built a complete email and SMS campaign system. You understand deliverability, the 4 campaign types, copywriting, design, platform building, compliance, segmentation, and personalization. This lesson closes the loop. Analytics is how you know whether all of that work is producing results. And more importantly, it is how you know exactly what to fix when it is not. Data without interpretation is just numbers. Interpretation without action is just observation. This lesson gives you the complete framework. Which metrics matter, how to read them correctly, how to diagnose problems using the metrics chain, and how to run the optimization loop that makes every campaign better than the last. Let's get into it. Before we look at any specific metric, you need to understand that not all numbers carry equal weight. There is a hierarchy, and optimizing toward the wrong level produces confident wrong decisions. At the bottom of the hierarchy are vanity metrics. Total subscribers, total emails sent, impressions. These numbers feel good when they grow, but they do not tell you whether your campaigns are working. A list of 10,000 subscribers that generates no revenue is not a success. It is a cost center. In the middle are performance metrics, open rate, CTR, CTOR, bounce rate, unsubscribe rate. These tell you how well your campaign executed, whether people opened it, whether they clicked, whether the delivery was clean. Performance metrics are diagnostic tools. They help you understand why business outcomes are high or low. At the top, the narrowest point of the pyramid are business outcome metrics. Conversion rate, revenue per email, cost per acquisition, customer lifetime value influenced by campaigns. These are what clients pay you to move. These are the numbers that appear in client reports. These are the numbers that justify your fees. Here is the principle that governs every analytics decision you make. A campaign with a 45% open rate and zero conversions is a failure. A campaign with 18% open rate and 12 purchases is a success. Never let a performance metric convince you a campaign worked when the business outcome metric says otherwise. Always report on business outcomes. Use performance metrics only to diagnose why those outcomes are what they are. Let me now walk through every email metric you need to understand. What it measures, what the benchmark looks like, and what a low or concerning number tells you about where your campaign broke. Open rate measures the percentage of delivered emails that were opened. The 2025 industry average is 43%, though Apple Mail privacy protection inflates this artificially. Use open rate as a directional trend indicator, not an absolute measurement. A consistently falling open rate is a signal worth investigating. An isolated low open rate on one campaign may mean nothing. CTR measures the percentage of delivered emails that generated a click. The healthy range for general campaigns is 2-5% and 5-10% for triggered and segmented campaigns. CTR is more reliable than open rate as a performance indicator because it is not affected by mail privacy protection. If your open rate is healthy but your CTR is low, the problem is in your email body or your CTA, not your subject line. CTOR, click-to-open rate, measures clicks only among those who actually open the email. The healthy range is 10-20%. A low CTOR with a decent open rate tells you that people opened the email but were not compelled by what they found inside. This is a body copy relevance problem, not a subject line problem. Conversion rate measures the percentage of delivered emails that resulted in the desired business action. A purchase, a booking, a sign up. The range for cold campaigns is 1-5%. For warm segmented campaigns it is 5-15%. A good CTR with a low conversion rate is a landing page problem, not an email problem. Follow the metrics chain before deciding what to fix. Unsubscribe rate measures the percentage of recipients who opted out after receiving the email. Below 0.5% is healthy. Above 1% requires immediate investigation. It signals content irrelevance the wrong campaign type reaching the wrong segment or sending frequency that exceeds what contacts are comfortable with Bounce Rate Measures Failed deliveries as a percentage of total cents. Below 2% is healthy. Above 5% is a list hygiene emergency. Hard bounces are permanent failures from invalid addresses. These must be removed from your list immediately after each campaign because every hard bounce left on your list chips away at your sender reputation. Spam complaint rate measures the percentage of recipients who mark your email as spam. Below 0.1% is the safe zone. Above 0.3% is a critical sender reputation emergency. At this level, mail providers begin blocking your emails proactively. A rising complaint rate almost always signals that contacts do not recognize you, your content feels irrelevant to them, or you are sending to contacts who did not give you explicit consent. Revenue per email is the ultimate business outcome metric. Divide the total revenue generated by a campaign by the number of emails delivered. Track this as a trend over time. It connects every performance decision directly to client revenue. SMS has fewer trackable metrics than email, but each one carries more weight because of how directly SMS reaches its audience. Delivery rate measures the percentage of messages that were successfully delivered to recipients. Above 95% is healthy. Below 90% demands investigation. The most common causes are invalid phone numbers on your list, carrier filtering due to an unregistered or low reputation sender number or an A2P 10 DLC registration issue from Lesson 6.7. SMS click-through rate measures the percentage of delivered messages that generated a link click. The benchmark is 19 to 35 percent, dramatically higher than email CTR because virtually every delivered SMS is read. A low SMS CTR with a healthy delivery rate means the message content is not compelling or the offer is not relevant to this specific segment. Opt-out rate measures the percentage of recipients who replied stop. Below 0.5% is healthy. Above 2% is a content or consent problem that requires immediate review. High opt-out rates are one of the fastest ways to damage SMS sender reputation because carriers track them directly. If your opt-out rate is rising, Review your consent quality, your message frequency, and your content relevance before sending another campaign. Response rate measures the percentage of recipients who replied to the SMS with any response beyond stop. For two-way conversation campaigns, a healthy range is 10-30%. Higher response rate contacts are prime candidates for direct personal follow-up. Flag them in Go High level and prioritize outreach. When diagnosing an SMS problem, follow the same chain logic as email. Low delivery rate means fix the sending infrastructure first. Low CTR with healthy delivery means fix the message content. High opt-out rate means fix the consent quality or the frequency. The metrics chain is the most practical tool in this lesson. It prevents the most expensive mistake in email optimization, fixing the wrong thing with confidence. The chain has five links. Deliverability. Did the email reach the inbox? Open. Did they open it? Engagement. Did the content hold their attention? Click. Did they click the CTA? Conversion. Did they take the desired action? Every campaign problem has a specific location in this chain, and the fix must match the breakpoint. Scenario number one. Low open rate combined with low CTR. This is a deliverability or subject line problem. The email is either not reaching the inbox at all or not earning the open once it arrives. Check your spam complaint rate and bounce rate first. If deliverability is clean, test a new subject line formula. Scenario number two, good open rate combined with low CTR. This is an email body copy or CTA problem. People are opening but not finding a compelling reason to click. The subject line did its job. Now go back to the hook context value CTA framework from lesson 6.3 and test a new CTA button text. Scenario number three, good CTR combined with low conversion. This is a landing page or offer problem, not an email problem. Your email did everything right. The destination is failing to convert. apply the landing page optimization framework from module 4 not email optimization scenario number 4 good open rate combined with high on subscribes this is a segmentation or relevance problem People are opening because the subject line is compelling but what they find inside is not what they expected or wanted. Review your segment criteria from lesson 6.8 and verify that the content matches what this audience actually needs at this stage. Follow the chain to find the break before you apply the fix. This single habit will save you from weeks of optimizing the wrong element. Analytics only creates value when it produces action. The optimization loop is the five-step process that turns every campaign result into a better next campaign. Step number one is send. Before you send any campaign, define your success criteria. What open rate, CTR, and conversion rate would make this campaign a success. Write it down before reviewing results. Without predefined criteria, every result gets rationalized after the fact. Step number two is measure. Pull all metrics from GHL statistics panel 48 to 72 hours after sending. This window allows enough time for most opens and clicks to register. Drawing conclusions at six hours produces unreliable data. Step number three is diagnose. Apply the metrics chain. Identify exactly which link broke and where the campaign underperformed against your predefined benchmarks. Do not proceed to hypothesizing until you have located the breakpoint. Step number four is hypothesize. Form one specific hypothesis about why the break occurred and what change would fix it. One variable, one hypothesis, per optimization cycle. I hypothesize that changing the subject line from direct benefit formula to curiosity gap formula will increase open rate by 10% or more. If you change three things at once, you cannot know which change produced the result. Step number five is optimize. Implement the specific change identified by your hypothesis. either in the next 10 of this campaign type or by setting up an AD test using the methodology covered in Module 8 Lesson 8.5. Then return to Step 1 and run the loop again. The optimization loop does not end. Every campaign teaches you something about your specific audience that no industry benchmark can tell you. The specialists who improve fastest are the ones who treat every send as both a revenue event and a data collection event simultaneously. Before we close this lesson, let me give you a benchmarks reference card you will use throughout your career. These numbers give you the industry context you need to evaluate whether a campaign result is strong, acceptable, or concerning. For email, the warning zones are open rate below 15%, CTR below 1%, CTOR below 5%, unsubscribe rate above 1%, bounce rate above 5%, and spam complaint rate above 0.3%. Healthy ranges sit between warning and excellent. Excellent performance is open rate 35% plus, CTR 5% plus, CTOR 20% plus, unsubscribe rate below 0.2%, bounce rate below 0.5%, spam complaints near zero. For SMS, the warning zones are delivery rate below 90%, CTR below 5%, opt-out rate above 2%, and response rate below 5%. Healthy SMS delivery sits at 95-98%, CTR at 15-25%, opt-out rate at 0.2-0.5%. Here is the principle that governs how you use these benchmarks. Industry benchmarks tell you where you stand relative to the market. Your own historical data tells you whether you are improving. Track both, but optimize against your own trend, not just the industry average. A 20% open rate that improved from 12% is a success story. A 35% open rate that declined from 45% requires investigation, regardless of how it compares to the industry average. Your audience is not the industry. Your benchmarks are your history. Let me close with six analytics mistakes that lead to wrong decisions. Because confident wrong decisions are more expensive than honest uncertainty. Mistake number one, focusing on open rate as your primary success metric. Apple Mail privacy protection inflates open rates artificially. A 55% open rate means very little if CTOR and conversion rate are poor. Fix. Use CTR and CTOR as your primary performance indicators They reflect genuine engagement that MPP does not affect Mistake number two Drawing conclusions from a single send One email performance is affected by send timing day of week current events and dozens of variables outside your control. A single data point is not a pattern. Fix. Look for trends across three to five sends of the same campaign type before making structural changes. Mistake number three, comparing campaigns with fundamentally different audiences. A campaign to 50 warm leads at 40% CTR and a campaign to 2,000 cold leads at 3% CTR are not comparable in any meaningful way. Fix, compare campaigns of similar audience size, segment type, and campaign type. Mistake number four, ignoring unsubscribe rate and spam complaint rate. These are your earliest warning systems for list health problems. By the time deliverability drops noticeably, unsubscribes and complaints have often been signaling the problem for weeks. Fix. Review these metrics after every send, not just open rate and CTR. Mistake number five. Not defining success criteria before sending. Without predefined benchmarks, every result gets rationalized as acceptable after the fact. Fix. Before every campaign, write down the specific numbers that constitute success. Then measure against that standard. Mistake number six. Attributing a conversion problem to the email when the issue is the landing page. A campaign with strong open rate and CTR but poor conversions worked. The problem is at the destination. Fix. Follow the metrics chain to find the break before applying any optimization. The chain tells you which element to fix. Ignore it and you risk spending weeks optimizing the wrong thing. The most expensive analytics mistake is fixing the wrong thing with confidence. The metrics chain exists specifically to prevent that. Let's lock in what you learned in this lesson. The metrics hierarchy runs from vanity metrics at the bottom, through performance metrics in the middle, to business outcome metrics at the top. Always report business outcome metrics to clients. Use performance metrics to diagnose why those outcomes are what they are. The eight email metrics to track are open rate, CTR, CTOR, conversion rate, unsubscribe rate, bounce rate, spam complaint rate, and revenue per email. Each answers a specific diagnostic question about where in the campaign chain a problem exists. The four SMS metrics to track are delivery rate, CTR, opt-out rate, and response rate. A delivery rate below 90% signals an A2P registration or carrier filtering issue that must be resolved before any other optimization is attempted. The metrics chain runs deliverability to open, to engagement, to click, to conversion. Every campaign problem has a specific location in this chain. Find the breakpoint before applying any fix. The optimization loop is send, measure, diagnose, hypothesize, optimize. Define success criteria before sending. Wait 48 to 72 hours before drawing conclusions. Form one specific hypothesis per optimization cycle. And treat every campaign as a learning event as well as a revenue event. The six most costly analytics mistakes are over-relying on open rate, drawing conclusions from single cents, ignoring unsubscribed and complaint rates, not defining success criteria in advance, comparing incomparable campaigns, and fixing the email when the conversion problem is on the landing page. Coming up next is the Module 6 hands-on practice. You have now completed all nine lessons in Module 6. You have the complete framework for creating effective email and SMS campaigns, from deliverability fundamentals all the way through analytics and optimization. The Module 6 hands-on practice is where you bring everything together into one integrated exercise, planning, building, and analytically evaluating a complete multi-channel campaign system using everything you learned across all nine lessons. This is your Module 6 portfolio piece. Let's finish the module strong. Let's complete module 6 with the hands-on practice. Thank you.

visualDescriptions.description:
The image presents a screenshot of an online course lesson, titled "Email and SMS Analytics," which appears to be part of a larger educational program. The lesson is divided into two main sections: a list of what you'll learn on the left and a graphic illustrating campaign performance on the right.

**Left Section: What You'll Learn**

*   **The metrics hierarchy** - which numbers are vanity, which measure performance, and which prove business outcomes
*   **Every email metric beyond open rate and CTR** - what each one means and what it tells you about your campaign
*   **The SMS metrics that determine whether your text campaigns are working**
*   **How to read GoHighLevel's analytics dashboard** to extract actionable insights
*   **How to diagnose exactly where a campaign is breaking down** using the metrics chain
*   **The optimization loop** - how to turn data into decisions and decisions into better campaigns

**Right Section: Campaign Performance**

*   A bar graph with a green bar indicating a 24% increase
*   Three tabs:
    *   **EMAIL METRICS**
    *   **SMS METRICS**
    *   **OPTIMIZE**

**Additional Elements**

*   **Estimated Time:** 14 Minutes
*   **Module 6 • Lesson 6.9**
*   **Libre** (logo in the top-right corner)

Overall, the image suggests that this lesson will provide learners with a comprehensive understanding of email and SMS analytics, including how to track and interpret data, diagnose campaign performance, and optimize future campaigns.

The image presents a summary of key takeaways from Lesson 6.9, focusing on metrics and optimization strategies for email and SMS campaigns.

*   **The Metrics Hierarchy**
    *   The Metrics Hierarchy consists of three levels: Vanity, Performance, and Business Outcome.
    *   It emphasizes the importance of reporting business outcomes to clients and using performance metrics to diagnose why outcomes are high or low.
*   **8 Email Metrics**
    *   The section lists eight email metrics, including Open Rate, Revenue Per Email, CTR, CTOR, Bounce, Unsubscribe, etc.
    *   These metrics help answer specific diagnostic questions about where the campaign problem exists.
*   **4 SMS Metrics**
    *   Four SMS metrics are identified: Track Delivery, CTR, Opt-Out, and Response.
    *   A delivery rate below 90% signals a critical A2P registration or carrier filtering issue that must be fixed first.
*   **The Metrics Chain**
    *   The Metrics Chain outlines a sequence of events: Deliverability → Open → Engagement → Click → Conversion.
    *   Every problem has a location; finding the break point in this chain before applying any solution is crucial.
*   **The Optimization Loop**
    *   The Optimization Loop involves a cyclical process: Send, Measure, Diagnose, Hypothesize, Optimize.
    *   It defines success pre-send, waits 48-72 hours, and tests only one specific hypothesis per cycle.
*   **6 Costly Mistakes**
    *   Six costly mistakes are highlighted, including over-focusing on opens, judging single sends, ignoring list health signals, lacking pre-defined criteria, bad comparisons, or fixing email when the landing page is the problem.
    *   Avoiding these mistakes is essential for effective campaign optimization.

In summary, the image provides a concise overview of key takeaways from Lesson 6.9, covering the Metrics Hierarchy, email and SMS metrics, the Metrics Chain, the Optimization Loop, and common costly mistakes to avoid. These concepts are essential for understanding and optimizing email and SMS campaigns effectively.

The image presents a simple yet striking design, featuring a bold message on a vibrant green background.

*   **Background**: 
    *   The background of the image is a solid green color.
    *   It fills the entire frame, providing a clean and minimalistic backdrop for the text.
*   **Text**:
    *   The text is centered in the image and reads "Freedom Through Knowledge" in large, white letters.
    *   The font used for the text is sans-serif and bold, making it easily readable.
    *   The text is positioned horizontally across the center of the image, drawing immediate attention to the message.

In summary, the image effectively conveys a powerful and straightforward message through its use of a bold, white font on a bright green background. The simplicity of the design ensures that the viewer's focus is drawn directly to the text, emphasizing the importance of the phrase "Freedom Through Knowledge."

The image presents a comprehensive overview of the metrics hierarchy, emphasizing that not all numbers are equal. It highlights the importance of tracking the right metrics to make informed decisions.

* **The Metrics Hierarchy: Not All Numbers Are Equal**
	+ The title suggests that the presentation will discuss the differences between various metrics and their significance.
* **Business Outcome Metrics**
	+ These metrics connect campaign performance to revenue.
	+ Examples: Conversion Rate, CPA, Revenue per Email, CLV, Pipeline Value
	+ Description: These are what clients pay you to move.
* **Performance Metrics**
	+ These metrics measure how well your campaign executed.
	+ Examples: Open Rate, CTR, CTOR, Deliverability, Bounce Rate, Opt-out Rate
	+ Description: Tells you HOW it performed, not WHAT it produced.
* **Vanity Metrics**
	+ These metrics feel good but do not drive decisions.
	+ Examples: Total Subscribers, Total Emails Sent, Social Shares, Impressions
	+ Description: Grow without necessarily meaning success.

**Right-hand side of the image:**

* **Primary Focus**
	+ Report on business outcome metrics to clients. This is the only level that proves ROI and justifies your retainer. Focus your monthly reporting here.
* **Diagnostic Tool**
	+ Use performance metrics to diagnose why outcomes are high or low. If revenue is down, check CTR and Open Rate to find the leak. These are for you, the specialist.
* **Health Check**
	+ Monitor vanity metrics only to catch anomalies. A sudden spike in subscribers or drop in sends might signal a technical issue, but they are not success indicators.

**Bottom of the image:**

* A campaign with 45% open rate and zero conversions is a failure. A campaign with 18% open rate and 12 purchases is a success. Never let a performance metric convince you a campaign worked when the business outcome says otherwise.

In summary, the image effectively illustrates the importance of prioritizing business outcome metrics, such as conversion rates and revenue per email, over performance metrics like open rates and click-through rates, and vanity metrics like total subscribers and social shares. By focusing on the right metrics, businesses can make informed decisions and achieve their goals. The image provides a clear and concise guide for marketers to understand the hierarchy of metrics and make data-driven decisions.

The image presents a comprehensive overview of key email metrics, providing insights into their meanings and implications. The title, "Every Email Metric - What It Means and What It Tells You," sets the stage for an in-depth exploration of email performance indicators.

Here is a detailed breakdown of the information presented:

* **Open Rate**
	+ Definition: (Opened ÷ Delivered) × 100
	+ Benchmark: 43% avg - Triggered: 40-70%
	+ Interpretation: A low open rate may indicate issues with the subject line, sender name, timing, or list hygiene problems.
* **Click-Through Rate (CTR)**
	+ Definition: (Clicks ÷ Delivered) × 100
	+ Benchmark: 2-5% General - 5-10% Triggered
	+ Interpretation: A low CTR with good opens suggests problems with body copy or CTAs, not subject lines.
* **Click-to-Open Rate (CTOR)**
	+ Definition: (Clicks ÷ Opened) × 100
	+ Benchmark: 10-20% healthy range
	+ Interpretation: A low CTOR indicates that openers weren't compelled, suggesting content relevance issues rather than subject line problems.
* **Conversion Rate**
	+ Definition: (Actions ÷ Delivered) × 100
	+ Benchmark: 1-5% Cold - 5-15% Warm
	+ Interpretation: A low conversion rate with good CTR implies landing page or offer problems, not email issues.
* **Unsubscribe Rate**
	+ Definition: (Unsubs ÷ Delivered) × 100
	+ Benchmark: <0.5% healthy - >1% alert
	+ Interpretation: A high unsubscribe rate may indicate irrelevance, wrong segment, or frequency issues.
* **Bounce Rate**
	+ Definition: (Bounced ÷ Sent) × 100
	+ Benchmark: <2% healthy - >5% emergency
	+ Interpretation: High hard bounces signify list hygiene crises, necessitating immediate removal of invalids.
* **Spam Complaint Rate**
	+ Definition: (Reports ÷ Delivered) × 100
	+ Benchmark: <0.1% safe - >0.3% critical
	+ Interpretation: Rising spam complaints indicate contacts don't recognize you or didn't consent, prompting cessation of sending.
* **Revenue Per Email**
	+ Definition: Total Revenue ÷ Delivered
	+ Benchmark: Varies by price point
	+ Interpretation: This metric connects every performance metric to actual client revenue.

In summary, the image provides a thorough analysis of essential email metrics, including open rates, click-through rates, conversion rates, unsubscribe rates, bounce rates, spam complaint rates, and revenue per email. By understanding these metrics and their benchmarks, marketers can identify areas for improvement and optimize their email campaigns for better performance.

The infographic, titled "SMS Analytics: The Metrics That Matter," presents a comprehensive overview of key performance indicators (KPIs) for SMS campaigns. The title is prominently displayed at the top, followed by a subtitle that reads, "Fewer metrics than email - but each one carries significant weight." The infographic is divided into two main sections: the top section focuses on five essential metrics, while the bottom section provides guidance on diagnosing common SMS problems.

**Top Section: Essential Metrics**

* **Delivery Rate**
	+ Definition: (Delivered ÷ Sent) × 100
	+ Benchmark: >95% healthy, <90% alert
	+ Low delivery rate may indicate invalid numbers, carrier filtering, or unregistered sender number (A2P 10DLC)
* **Read Rate**
	+ Definition: Inferred Metric
	+ Benchmark: ~98% industry average
	+ Note: Not tracked directly by GHL; if delivery is high, assume read rate is near 98%
* **SMS CTR**
	+ Definition: (Clicks ÷ Delivered) × 100
	+ Benchmark: 19% to 35% typical
	+ Low CTR may indicate a message that is not compelling, a link that is not mobile-friendly, or an offer that is irrelevant to the segment
* **Response Rate**
	+ Definition: % of Replies (Any)
	+ Benchmark: 10-30% (Two-way campaigns)
	+ High response rate indicates strong engagement; these contacts are prime for personal follow-up
* **Opt-out Rate**
	+ Definition: (STOPS ÷ Delivered) × 100
	+ Benchmark: <0.5% healthy, >2% alert
	+ High opt-out rate may indicate sending too frequently, irrelevant content, or poor consent quality

**Bottom Section: SMS Problem Diagnosis**

* **Low Delivery Rate**
	+ Check A2P 10DLC registration status first; then check number validity and carrier filtering logs
* **Low Click-Through Rate**
	+ Review message content for clarity; ensure the offer is relevant and the destination link works perfectly on mobile
* **High Opt-Out Rate**
	+ Review consent quality (did they actually opt-in?); check if frequency is too high or content is annoying

In summary, the infographic provides a clear and concise guide to understanding the key metrics and potential issues that can arise in SMS campaigns. By monitoring these metrics and addressing any problems that arise, businesses can optimize their SMS marketing strategies and improve their overall performance.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to diagnosing and fixing issues in email campaigns, titled "The Metrics Chain: Diagnosing Exactly Where a Campaign Breaks." The infographic is divided into two main sections: a top section illustrating the metrics chain and a bottom section providing diagnostic scenarios.

**Top Section: Metrics Chain**

*   The metrics chain consists of five interconnected boxes, each representing a stage in the email campaign process:
    *   **Deliverability**: Did the email reach the inbox? (Delivered, Bounce, Spam %)
    *   **Open**: Did they open the email? (Open Rate)
    *   **Engagement**: Did content hold attention? (CTOR, Scroll Depth)
    *   **Click**: Did they click the CTA? (CTR)
    *   **Conversion**: Did they take action? (Conv. Rate, Revenue)

**Bottom Section: Diagnostic Scenarios**

*   This section provides four diagnostic scenarios, each describing a common issue and its corresponding solution:
    *   **Low Open Rate + Low CTR**: Deliverability or subject line problem. Email is not reaching inbox or not earning the open.
        *   Fix: Check spam/bounce rates first. If healthy, test new subject line.
    *   **Good Open Rate + Low CTR**: Email body copy or CTA problem. People are opening but not finding a reason to click.
        *   Fix: Review Body Framework. Test new CTA text.
    *   **Good CTR + Low Conversion**: Landing page or offer problem. Email is doing its job; destination is not converting.
        *   Fix: Not an email problem. Optimize Landing Page (Module 4).
    *   **Good Open + High Unsubs**: Segmentation or relevance problem. What they found was not what they wanted.
        *   Fix: Review segmentation criteria (Lesson 6.8).

**Additional Information**

*   A black banner at the bottom of the image provides additional context:
    *   The metrics chain prevents the most common optimization mistake: fixing the wrong thing. A low conversion rate is not always an email problem. A high unsubscribe rate is not always a copy problem. Follow the chain to find the break before applying the fix.

In summary, the image provides a clear and concise guide to understanding and addressing common issues in email campaigns. By following the metrics chain and diagnostic scenarios, marketers can identify and fix problems at each stage of the campaign process, ultimately improving the effectiveness of their email marketing efforts.

The image presents a comprehensive infographic titled "The Metrics Chain: Diagnosing Exactly Where a Campaign Breaks," which provides a step-by-step approach to identifying and resolving issues in email marketing campaigns. The infographic is divided into two main sections: the top section illustrates the Metrics Chain, while the bottom section outlines various diagnosis scenarios.

**Metrics Chain:**

*   **Deliverability**
    *   Did the email reach the inbox?
    *   Delivered, Bounce, Spam %
*   **Open**
    *   Did they open the email?
    *   Open Rate
*   **Engagement**
    *   Did content hold attention?
    *   CTOR, Scroll Depth
*   **Click**
    *   Did they click the CTA?
    *   CTR
*   **Conversion**
    *   Did they take action?
    *   Conv. Rate, Revenue

**What the Numbers Tell You (Diagnosis Scenarios):**

*   **Low Open Rate + Low CTR**
    *   Deliverability or subject line problem. Email is not reaching inbox or not earning the open.
    *   Fix: Check spam/bounce rates first. If healthy, test new subject line.
*   **Good Open Rate + Low CTR**
    *   Email body copy or CTA problem. People are opening but not finding a reason to click.
    *   Fix: Review Body Framework. Test new CTA text.
*   **Good CTR + Low Conversion**
    *   Landing page or offer problem. Email is doing its job; destination is not converting.
    *   Fix: Not an email problem. Optimize Landing Page (Module 4).
*   **Good Open + High Unsubs**
    *   Segmentation or relevance problem. What they found was not what they wanted.
    *   Fix: Review segmentation criteria (Lesson 6.8).

The infographic concludes with a black box containing white text that reads, "The metrics chain prevents the most common optimization mistake: fixing the wrong thing. A low conversion rate is not always an email problem. A high unsubscribe rate is not always a copy problem. Follow the chain to find the break before applying the fix." This emphasizes the importance of using the Metrics Chain to accurately diagnose and address issues in email marketing campaigns.

The image presents a flowchart titled "The Optimization Loop: From Data to Better Campaigns" with the subtitle "Analytics is only valuable if it produces action - the optimization loop turns every campaign into a learning opportunity." The flowchart illustrates the continuous improvement process.

*   **Title and Subtitle**
    *   The title is in large black text at the top of the image.
    *   The subtitle is in smaller gray text below the title.
*   **Flowchart**
    *   The flowchart consists of five main steps: Send, Measure, Diagnose, Hypothesize, and Optimize.
    *   Each step has a brief description and a rule associated with it.
    *   The steps are connected by dotted lines, indicating a cyclical process.
*   **Main Steps**
    *   **Send**
        *   Launch the campaign with clear success metrics defined in advance.
        *   Rule: Define success criteria BEFORE sending.
    *   **Measure**
        *   Pull all metrics from GHL stats panel 48-72 hours after send.
        *   Rule: Wait at least 48 hours for data stabilization.
    *   **Diagnose**
        *   Apply the metrics chain to identify which link broke.
        *   Rule: Find the break point before deciding the fix.
    *   **Hypothesize**
        *   Form one specific hypothesis about what change will fix the break.
        *   Rule: Test one variable per optimization cycle.
    *   **Optimize**
        *   Implement the change. Return to Step 1 with revised campaign.
        *   Rule: Apply changes to future sends or A/B tests.
*   **Continuous Improvement**
    *   The center of the flowchart features a dark blue circle with a green arrow icon and the words "CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT" in white text.
*   **Footer**
    *   A black bar at the bottom of the image contains white text that reads, "The optimization loop does not end. The specialists who improve fastest are the ones who treat every send as a data collection event, not just a revenue event."

In summary, the image provides a clear and concise visual representation of the optimization loop, highlighting the importance of continuous improvement in campaign optimization. By following these steps, marketers can turn every campaign into a learning opportunity and drive better results over time.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to email and SMS analytics benchmarks, providing valuable insights for businesses and marketers. The title "Email and SMS Analytics Benchmarks - Quick Reference" sets the tone for the infographic, which is divided into two main sections: Email Benchmarks and SMS Benchmarks.

**Email Benchmarks**

* **Metric**: A list of key metrics used to evaluate email performance
	+ Open Rate
	+ CTR (Click-Through Rate)
	+ CTOR (Click-to-Open Rate)
	+ Unsubscribe
	+ Bounce Rate
	+ Complaint Rate
* **Warning**: Thresholds indicating potential issues with email performance
	+ Open Rate: <15%
	+ CTR: <1%
	+ CTOR: <5%
	+ Unsubscribe: >1%
	+ Bounce Rate: >5%
	+ Complaint Rate: >0.3%
* **Healthy**: Benchmarks for optimal email performance
	+ Open Rate: 20%-35%
	+ CTR: 2%-5%
	+ CTOR: 10%-20%
	+ Unsubscribe: 0.2%-0.5%
	+ Bounce Rate: <2%
	+ Complaint Rate: <0.1%
* **Excellent**: High-performing email metrics
	+ Open Rate: 35%+
	+ CTR: 5%+
	+ CTOR: 20%+
	+ Unsubscribe: <0.2%
	+ Bounce Rate: <0.5%
	+ Complaint Rate: ~0%

**SMS Benchmarks**

* **Metric**: A list of key metrics used to evaluate SMS performance
	+ Delivery Rate
	+ CTR (Click-Through Rate)
	+ Opt-Out Rate
	+ Response Rate
* **Warning**: Thresholds indicating potential issues with SMS performance
	+ Delivery Rate: <90%
	+ CTR: <5%
	+ Opt-Out Rate: >2%
	+ Response Rate: <5%
* **Healthy**: Benchmarks for optimal SMS performance
	+ Delivery Rate: 95%-98%
	+ CTR: 15%-25%
	+ Opt-Out Rate: 0.2%-0.5%
	+ Response Rate: 10%-20%
* **Excellent**: High-performing SMS metrics
	+ Delivery Rate: 98%+
	+ CTR: 25%+
	+ Opt-Out Rate: <0.2%
	+ Response Rate: 20%+

**Additional Insights**

* **Note**: Triggered and segmented emails consistently outperform these general benchmarks.
* **Note**: Transactional SMS consistently outperforms promotional SMS on all metrics.

**Industry Benchmarks**

* **Industry benchmarks tell you where you stand relative to the market. Your own historical data tells you whether you are improving. Track both - but optimize against your own trend, not just the industry average.**
* **Example:** A 20% open rate that improved from 12% is a success. A 35% open rate that declined from 45% requires investigation.

In summary, the image provides a detailed breakdown of email and SMS analytics benchmarks, including metrics, warning thresholds, healthy benchmarks, and excellent performance indicators. By understanding these benchmarks, businesses and marketers can evaluate their performance, identify areas for improvement, and optimize their strategies to achieve better results.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to avoiding common analytics mistakes that can lead to incorrect decisions. The title, "6 Analytics Mistakes That Lead to Wrong Decisions," sets the tone for the infographic, which provides valuable insights into the pitfalls of data interpretation.

**Mistakes and Corrections**

The infographic outlines six critical errors in data analysis, each accompanied by a brief explanation and a suggested correction:

*   **Focusing on Open Rate as Primary Success Metric**
    *   Why it hurts: Apple MPP inflates open rates artificially. A 55% open rate means nothing if CTOR and conversions are poor.
    *   THE FIX: Use CTR and CTOR as your primary performance indicators - they reflect genuine engagement.
*   **Drawing Conclusions from a Single Send**
    *   Why it hurts: One-off variables (day of week, news events) distort results. A single data point is not a pattern.
    *   THE FIX: Look for trends across 3 to 5 sends of the same campaign type before making structural changes.
*   **Comparing Campaigns with Different Audiences**
    *   Why it hurts: A campaign to 50 warm leads vs 2,000 cold leads are not comparable. Quality varies drastically.
    *   THE FIX: Only compare campaigns with similar audience sizes, segment types, and goals.
*   **Ignoring Unsubscribe & Spam Complaint Rates**
    *   Why it hurts: These are early warnings. By the time deliverability drops, these have often been signaling danger for weeks.
    *   THE FIX: Review unsubscribe and complaint rates after every single send, not just opens/clicks.
*   **Not Defining Success Criteria Before Sending**
    *   Why it hurts: Without benchmarks, poor results get rationalized as "acceptable" after the fact.
    *   THE FIX: Define target Open, CTR, and Conversion rates pre-send. Measure strictly against that standard.
*   **Blaming Email When the Problem is the Landing Page**
    *   Why it hurts: Strong open/CTR with poor conversions is a destination problem. Tweaking the email won't fix it.
    *   THE FIX: Follow the Metrics Chain. If they clicked, the email did its job. Optimize the landing page.

**Conclusion**

The infographic concludes with a crucial reminder: "The most expensive analytics mistake is fixing the wrong thing with confidence. The metrics chain from this lesson exists specifically to prevent that. Follow it before every optimization decision." This emphasizes the importance of careful data analysis and interpretation to ensure informed decision-making.
